perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by, and construed in accordance with, the laws of the State of ____________________ without giving effect to any conflicts or choice of laws provisions that would cause the application of the domestic substantive laws of any other jurisdiction.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by, and construed in accordance with, the laws of the State of ____________________, giving effect to any conflicts or choice of laws provisions that would cause the application of the laws of any jurisdiction the Parties select.
perturbation.explanation: This modification allows the parties to potentially circumvent consumer protection laws by choosing a jurisdiction with weaker protections, even if the transaction or the parties themselves are primarily located in a state with stronger consumer laws. It contradicts the principle that consumer protection laws should apply based on the consumer's location or the transaction's primary location, irrespective of contractual choice of law clauses.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Choice of Law rules and Consumer Protection Statutes
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC 1-301 allows parties to choose the law governing their agreement, but this choice is not absolute.  Consumer protection laws, particularly those designed to protect vulnerable parties, may override choice of law provisions if the chosen law would deprive the consumer of protections they would otherwise receive under the law of the state where they reside or where the transaction primarily occurs. This change attempts to bypass such protections.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 1-301. Territorial Applicability; Parties' Power to Choose Applicable Law. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 1-301. Territorial Applicability; Parties' Power to Choose Applicable Law. a) Except as otherwise provided in this section, when a transaction bears a reasonable relation to this state and also to another state or nation the parties may agree that the law either of this state or of such other state or nation shall govern their rights and duties. (b) In the absence of an agreement effective under subsection (a), and except as provided in subsection (c), [the Uniform Commercial Code] applies to transactions bearing an appropriate relation to this state. (c) If one of the following provisions of [the Uniform Commercial Code] specifies the applicable law, that provision governs and a contrary agreement is effective only to the extent permitted by the law so specified: (1) Section 2-402 ; (2) Sections 2A-105 and 2A-106 ; (3) Section 4-102 ; (4) Section 4A-507 ; (5) Section 5-116 ; [(6) Section 6-103 ; (7) Section 8-110 ; (8) Sections 9-301 through 9 - 307 . perturbation.justification: The changed text allows parties to select a jurisdiction whose laws govern the agreement, potentially circumventing consumer protection laws of the state where the transaction primarily occurs or where the consumer resides. This directly contradicts the principle that certain consumer protection laws should apply irrespective of contractual choice of law. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing UCC 1-301, allows parties to choose the law governing their agreement. However, the Perturbation and Law Explanations indicate that this choice is not absolute, particularly where consumer protection is concerned, as the chosen law cannot deprive consumers of protections they would otherwise receive. The modification removes a restriction on choice of law that prevents circumvention of consumer protection laws, creating a clear contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Each Recipient acknowledges that a violation of this Agreement would cause irreparable harm to the Discloser for which no adequate remedy at law exists and each Recipient therefore agrees that, in addition to any other remedies available, both Parties will be entitled to seek injunctive relief to enforce the terms of this Agreement without a requirement that Discloser prove irreparable harm or provide the posting of a bond.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Recipient acknowledges that a violation of this Agreement would cause harm to the Discloser and each Recipient therefore agrees that, in addition to any other remedies available, both Parties will be entitled to seek relief to enforce the terms of this Agreement with a requirement that Discloser prove harm and provide the posting of a bond.
perturbation.explanation: This change removes the acknowledgement that a breach would cause irreparable harm, which is a key element for obtaining injunctive relief. By adding a requirement that the Discloser prove harm and post a bond, the clause makes it significantly more difficult and expensive for the Discloser to obtain an injunction, effectively weakening their ability to enforce the confidentiality provisions. This affects trade secret protections.
perturbation.law_explanation: The DTSA allows for injunctive relief to prevent trade secret misappropriation. While the statute doesn't explicitly prohibit requiring a bond, making it unduly difficult to obtain an injunction can undermine the effectiveness of the DTSA, especially when irreparable harm is likely (as is often the case with trade secrets).

perturbation.justification: The original contract acknowledges irreparable harm to the Discloser in case of a breach, which is a significant factor in obtaining injunctive relief. The changed text removes this acknowledgement and adds a requirement that the Discloser prove harm and post a bond, which undermines the availability of injunctive relief. The Law Explanation states that making it unduly difficult to obtain an injunction can undermine the effectiveness of the DTSA, especially when irreparable harm is likely. Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 is empty, Scraped Law Snippet 2 refers to the Defend Trade Secrets Act of 2016 (DTSA), which provides for injunctive relief to prevent trade secret misappropriation, as the Law Explanation also states. By making it more difficult for the Discloser to obtain an injunction, the changed text contradicts the intent of the DTSA to provide effective remedies for trade secret misappropriation, thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient agrees that it shall take reasonable measures to protect the secrecy of and avoid disclosure and unauthorized use of the Confidential Information. Without limiting the foregoing, Recipient shall take at least those measures that it takes to protect its own confidential information of a similar nature, but in no event less than reasonable measures.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient agrees that it shall take measures to protect the secrecy of and avoid disclosure and unauthorized use of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the phrase "at least those measures that it takes to protect its own confidential information of a similar nature, but in no event less than reasonable measures" significantly weakens the standard of care required of the Recipient. This creates a legal risk because it eliminates a benchmark for evaluating the Recipient's efforts to protect the confidential information. Depending on the state, this change lowers the required level of protection below what is legally acceptable.
perturbation.law_explanation: UTSA requires 'reasonable efforts' to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. Removing the comparative standard (measures taken to protect its own confidential information) and the floor ('no event less than reasonable measures') provides no clear benchmark for what is considered acceptable, potentially falling short of the 'reasonable efforts' standard required for trade secret protection.

perturbation.justification: The omission of the phrase "at least those measures that it takes to protect its own confidential information of a similar nature, but in no event less than reasonable measures" weakens the standard of care. The Law Explanation states UTSA requires 'reasonable efforts'. The modified text fails to provide a clear benchmark, potentially falling short of the 'reasonable efforts' standard. The absence of a snippet does not invalidate the generated law explanation, which indicates a potential contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: At the request of the Discloser, Recipient will promptly return to the Discloser or at Discloser’s instructions destroy any written or electronic Confidential Information and all physical media on which Confidential Information was received from the other Recipient, including any copies thereof, with a letter confirming that the Confidential Information has not been reproduced or copied or that all copies have been returned and that none of the Confidential Information was disclosed to anyone except in accordance with this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: At the request of the Discloser, Recipient will return to the Discloser or destroy any Confidential Information and all physical media on which Confidential Information was received from the other Recipient, including any copies thereof.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the phrase "with a letter confirming that the Confidential Information has not been reproduced or copied or that all copies have been returned and that none of the Confidential Information was disclosed to anyone except in accordance with this Agreement", the modified clause eliminates the requirement for the Recipient to provide written confirmation of compliance. This makes it more difficult for the Discloser to verify that the Recipient has actually returned or destroyed all Confidential Information and has not misused it, undermining the protection of trade secrets.
perturbation.law_explanation: While California Trade Secret Law doesn't explicitly mandate a written confirmation upon return of confidential information, the lack of a verification mechanism makes it harder for the Discloser to demonstrate misappropriation, which is a key element in a trade secret claim. This weakens the Discloser's ability to enforce their rights under the law. A letter of confirmation would serve as evidence that the Recipient took steps to protect the trade secret.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426.2 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.2. (a) Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined. Upon application to the court, an injunction shall be terminated when the trade secret has ceased to exist, but the injunction may be continued for an additional period of time in order to eliminate commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation. (b) If the court determines that it would be unreasonable to prohibit future use, an injunction may condition future use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time the use could have been prohibited. (c) In appropriate circumstances, affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The modified contract omits a requirement for the Recipient to provide written confirmation that the Confidential Information has not been reproduced, copied, or disclosed. The Law Explanation states that this lack of verification makes it harder for the Discloser to demonstrate misappropriation, a key element in a trade secret claim. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing California Civil Code 3426.2, discusses enjoining actual or threatened misappropriation. While it doesn't explicitly mandate a written confirmation, the absence of a verification mechanism in the modified contract weakens the Discloser's ability to enforce their rights against misappropriation, thus contradicting the intention of trade secret law to protect against such misappropriation. The original text provided a means to confirm compliance, which is now absent, creating a legal loophole.
